positive_experience.situation: When a patient presents with redness, swelling, and pus around a nail, and the history suggests recent trauma or nail-biting, but no imaging or lab results are available, what should the doctor do next?
positive_experience.action: Update the differential to include paronychia and confirm the diagnosis with targeted patient history about trauma or nail manipulation, then finalize only after ruling out onychia or other nail infections.
positive_experience.boundary: Use this memory when symptoms suggest paronychia and no imaging or labs are available; do not use if there is evidence of nail bed involvement or if the patient has systemic signs of infection.

positive_experience.situation: When hip pain is described as aching and localized with stiffness and functional limitation, but no imaging or labs are available, what should the doctor avoid?
positive_experience.action: Avoid finalizing diagnosis without imaging or physical exam findings, as this may lead to premature closure and miss alternative diagnoses like infection or fracture.
positive_experience.boundary: Use when imaging or physical exam is missing; do not use if imaging is available and shows clear osteoarthritis or if the patient has systemic symptoms suggesting infection or malignancy.

positive_experience.situation: When a patient presents with disorganized speech, flat affect, and social withdrawal without any lab or imaging results, and the history strongly suggests a psychotic disorder, what should the doctor do next?
positive_experience.action: Update the differential to include schizophrenia and request targeted imaging or lab tests only if clinically indicated, but prioritize focused history and mental status exam to confirm core features.
positive_experience.boundary: Use when symptoms suggest psychosis but no imaging or labs are available; do not use if there is a clear alternative diagnosis or if imaging/labs are urgently needed for safety.

positive_experience.situation: When a patient presents with localized aching hip pain that worsens with movement and is associated with morning stiffness and functional limitation, but no prior history or lab results are available, what should the doctor do next?
positive_experience.action: Request a focused physical exam to assess joint mobility, crepitus, and gait, then review imaging if available to confirm joint space narrowing or osteophytes.
positive_experience.boundary: Use when pain is localized, mechanical, and associated with stiffness; do not use if there is systemic inflammation, trauma history, or imaging showing fracture or infection.

positive_experience.situation: When a 75-year-old female presents with pelvic pressure, bloating, urinary frequency, and dyspareunia, and initial labs and imaging are unavailable, the doctor should update the hypothesis to consider malignancy as a default assumption.
positive_experience.action: Update the differential diagnosis to include malignancy as a default assumption, even in the absence of imaging or lab results, to avoid premature closure.
positive_experience.boundary: Use this memory when facing a pelvic mass in an elderly woman with no imaging or lab results; do not use if imaging or lab results are available and confirm a benign diagnosis.

negative_experience.situation: When a patient presents with fever, weight loss, and poor appetite without specific localizing symptoms, and initial history and lab review yield no evidence, the doctor should avoid premature diagnosis of malignancy without imaging or targeted investigation.
negative_experience.action: Avoid finalizing diagnosis of malignancy without reviewing relevant imaging (e.g., renal or urinary tract ultrasound) or obtaining specific lab tests (e.g., urinalysis, creatinine, calcium) to rule out calculi or other non-malignant causes.
negative_experience.boundary: Use this memory when systemic symptoms like fever, weight loss, and poor appetite are present without localizing signs, and imaging or lab tests are unavailable or not requested; do not use if imaging (e.g., ultrasound) or specific labs (e.g., urinalysis) are available and not reviewed.

negative_experience.situation: When a patient presents with non-specific symptoms and no clear history, and initial targeted history and lab requests yield no findings, the doctor should avoid premature reliance on imaging without first considering toxicology or behavioral history.
negative_experience.action: Avoid requesting imaging like CXR without first exploring toxicology or behavioral history, especially when symptoms are non-specific and no lab results are available.
negative_experience.boundary: Use this memory when symptoms are non-specific and no lab results are available; do not use if toxicology or behavioral history has already been explored or if imaging is indicated by clinical suspicion.

negative_experience.situation: When a patient presents with constipation followed by diarrhea with mucus, cramping, syncope, and ankle pain, especially in an elderly female, and initial labs are unavailable, the doctor should avoid premature diagnosis without imaging or targeted physical exam.
negative_experience.action: Request imaging of the abdomen and pelvis to evaluate for bowel obstruction or diverticulitis, and perform a focused physical exam including assessment of distension, tenderness, and pulses in the lower extremities.
negative_experience.boundary: Use this memory when the patient has constipation followed by diarrhea with mucus, cramping, syncope, and ankle pain, and when labs are unavailable or inconclusive; do not use if imaging is contraindicated or if the patient has clear signs of fracture on physical exam or prior imaging.

arguments.question: Can you describe the right leg redness and painful nodule in more detail? For example, when did it start, how big is it, and has it changed over time?

clinical_turn.patient_or_tool_response: {'question': 'The redness on my right leg started near my groin and went down past my knee to my lower leg — it was a bright pink, nonblanching area that felt warm to the touch. I also had a 1 cm tender, mobile nodule on the medial thigh that was painful to press on. The redness has resolved now, but the nodule is still there — it’s about 0.25 cm in size and still slightly tender. I’m not sure if it’s gotten bigger or smaller since I first noticed it, but it hasn’t changed much.'}

case_memory.chief_complaint: Right leg redness and painful nodule
current_turn_information: Redness on right leg started near groin, extended past knee to lower leg
Bright pink, nonblanching, warm to touch
1 cm tender, mobile nodule on medial thigh, painful to press
Redness resolved, nodule still present, now 0.25 cm, slightly tender
case_memory.prior_information_summary: 58-year-old female with advanced IGG kappa multiple myeloma, amyloidosis, and paroxysmal SVT on apixaban. Presented with likely cellulitis. Six days ago: fever 101-102, chills, cramps, poor appetite. Worsening left rib pain (fracture), painful nodule in right medial thigh. No rash initially. Symptoms except rib pain and nodule resolved. Did not take revlimid due to fever. On admission: red rash on right inner thigh. Blood cultures, urine cultures, flu swab, respiratory viral panel taken. Since admission: denies fever/chills, rash has gotten bigger (now resolved), nodule still tender. No swelling, upper respiratory symptoms, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or urine changes. Appetite good. Last seen in heme/onc for daratumamab (tolerated well). Missed cardiology follow-up. Physical exam: erythematous region in medial thigh, nonblanching bright pink macules, warm, tender 1 cm subcutaneous nodule. Final result: cellulitis of right lower limb.

query.query_text: Right leg redness resolved but tender nodule persists; need memory about differentials beyond cellulitis; avoid premature diagnosis; consider imaging review if nodule changes

case_memory.chief_complaint: Right leg redness and painful nodule
current_turn_information: The redness on my right leg started near my groin and went down past my knee to my lower leg — it was a bright pink, nonblanching area that felt warm to the touch.
I also had a 1 cm tender, mobile nodule on the medial thigh that was painful to press on.
The redness has resolved now, but the nodule is still there — it’s about 0.25 cm in size and still slightly tender.
I’m not sure if it’s gotten bigger or smaller since I first noticed it, but it hasn’t changed much.
case_memory.prior_information_summary: 58-year-old female with advanced IGG kappa multiple myeloma, amyloidosis, and paroxysmal SVT on apixaban. Presented with likely cellulitis: fever, chills, cramps, poor appetite, worsening left rib pain (fracture), and painful nodule in right medial thigh. No rash initially, but red rash noted on right inner thigh during PET visit. Blood cultures, urine cultures, flu swab, and respiratory viral panel taken. Denies fever/chills since admission, denies other rashes, swelling, or systemic symptoms. Appetite good. History includes multiple myeloma, amyloidosis, thyroid nodules, atrial flutter, neuropathy, cataracts, and hearing loss. Family history: no myeloma, mother with arthritis, father with CVA, brother with hypertension, sister with diabetes. Physical exam: erythematous region in medial thigh extending from groin to distal lower extremity, nonblanching bright pink macules, warm to touch, tender 1 cm subcutaneous nodule. Discharge exam: erythema resolved, nodule slightly tender, 0.25 cm, mobile. Final result: Cellulitis of right lower limb.

case_memory.chief_complaint: Right leg redness and painful nodule
current_turn_information: I had a fever and chills six days ago, along with body and face cramps, which improved over the day.
I also had worsening left rib pain (from a fracture earlier this year) and a painful nodule in my right thigh.
I didn’t take my revlimid on ___ because of the fever and chills.
I didn’t have any other recent infections, surgeries, or exposure to new medications or substances — except for my scheduled daratumamab infusion, which I missed because I was admitted.
I’m not sure if that counts as a new substance, but I didn’t start any new meds or substances since my last clinic visit.
case_memory.prior_information_summary: 58-year-old female with advanced IGG kappa multiple myeloma, amyloidosis, and paroxysmal SVT on apixaban. Presented with likely cellulitis. Six days ago: fever 101-102, shaking chills, body/face cramps, poor appetite. Worsening left rib pain (fracture this year) and painful nodule in right medial thigh. No rash at that time. Symptoms except rib pain and nodule resolved. Did not take revlimid due to fever/chills. On admission: red rash on right inner thigh noticed during PET. Blood cultures, urine cultures, flu swab, respiratory viral panel taken. Since admission: denies fever/chills; thinks rash on leg has gotten bigger; not itchy; only area that hurts is nodule in right inner thigh; denies other rashes, swelling, upper respiratory symptoms, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or urine changes. Appetite good. Last seen in heme/onc for follow-up and next dose of daratumamab (tolerated well). Missed cardiology follow-up due to admission. Physical exam: erythematous region in medial thigh extending from groin to distal lower extremity, nonblanching bright pink macules, warm to touch, tender 1 cm subcutaneous nodule on palpation. Discharge exam: erythema resolved, slightly tender 0.25 cm subcutaneous nodule. Final result: Cellulitis of right lower limb.

content.outcome: Imaging would have revealed the true diagnosis (cellulitis vs. DVT) and prevented premature closure on a potentially dangerous misdiagnosis.
